raw_case:
Dacryocystitis Secondary to Orbital Mesh Implant Impingement: A Rare Etiology
A 22-year-old male patient reported swelling in relation to the right eye and developed recurrent purulent discharge and epiphora following a reconstructive traumatic orbital floor fracture repair two years ago. Radiographic investigation and surgical exploration reveal obstruction of the lacrimal apparatus at the lacrimal sac level due to over-extension/migration of the orbital floor mesh. The migration of the mesh was probably due to the extension of the mesh medially into the paranasal region.

Introduction

An inflammatory condition of the nasolacrimal sac is known as dacryocystitis. It is usually due to obstruction in the nasolacrimal duct, which causes the tears to stagnate in the lacrimal apparatus.Dacryocystitis is a potentially dangerous illness marked by acute infection and inflammation of the lacrimal apparatus, and it is defined as "a medical urgency that is clinically characterized by the rapid onset of pain, erythema, and swelling, classically below the medial canthal tendon, with or without preexisting epiphora, mainly resulting from the acute infection of the lacrimal sac and perisac tissues" [1].

Case presentation

A 22-year-old male reported to our Department of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery with a complaint of pain and swelling in relation to the right lower eyelid region for the past two weeks. The patient was apparently normal for two weeks, after which he developed a small swelling in relation to the lower eyelid. The swelling was initially small and gradually increased to its current size, accompanied by vague pain. He had consulted multiple hospitals for the same and was prescribed basic analgesics, antibiotic eye drops, and oral antibiotics. Despite treatment, his symptoms rapidly worsened.The patient had a previous history of trauma due to a road traffic accident and was diagnosed with a fronto-naso-orbito-ethmoidal fracture, for which he was treated with open reduction and internal fixation, orbital exploration, and orbital floor reconstruction under general anesthesia two years ago.On examination, the patient had a solitary swelling measuring 6 cm x 4 cm in the right lower eyelid region. An erythematic, voluminous mass was localized just inferior to the medial canthus of the right eye, covering most of the eye and causing obstruction to vision (Figure 1). On palpation, it was tender, fluctuant, and warm. The patient had a skin fistulous communication and punctal leakage of purulent discharge upon applying pressure. The patient did not suffer any recent trauma, associated fever, or signs of conjunctivitis. Vision was normal, and extraocular eye movements were free and full. An ophthalmic opinion was obtained, and an implant site infection was suspected.Following this, the patient was immediately admitted and started on intravenous antibiotics (Inj cefotaxime 1 g, Inj metrogyl 500 mg), and a pus culture was sent, which turned out to be sterile.High-resolution computed tomography was performed, which revealed medial impingement of the orbital mesh over the nasolacrimal duct apparatus; obvious obliteration was noted. A treatment plan for orbital exploration and orbital mesh removal under general anesthesia was suggested.The patient was taken up for surgery, intubated nasoendotracheally, and general anesthesia was induced following standard protocols. The patient was painted, prepared, and draped. Through the existing scar tissue at the previous midtarsal incision site, a blunt dissection was carried out to reach the bony septum, orbital rim, and orbital floor. A well-adapted mesh and floor were noted; while attempting to remove the mesh, the operating surgeon encountered difficulty in removing it from the medial aspect because the medial flange was overextended and had fused with the medial nasal bone, compromising the patency of the nasolacrimal duct. The fused part of the mesh was removed in two pieces. A thorough metrogyl wash and irrigation were given, and layer-wise closure was achieved using Vicryl 4-0 and Ethilon 4-0 (Ethicon, Somerville, NJ, USA). The patient was extubated, and recovery was uneventful. Sutures were removed after one week, and healing was satisfactory.One month postoperatively, the patient returned for review with swelling in the same region, indicating a rupture of the soft tissue duct and the need for repair. The patient was then referred to an ophthalmic center for nasolacrimal duct repair.

Discussion

An inflammatory condition of the nasolacrimal sac is known as dacryocystitis. It is usually due to obstruction in the nasolacrimal duct, which causes tears to stagnate in the lacrimal apparatus. Clinically, dacryocystitis is recognized by swelling and inflammation of the lacrimal sac at the inferomedial canthus [2].A better understanding of dacryocystitis and its possible multilayer involvement arises from knowledge of the anatomy of the apparatus and the flow of tears. When tears are produced by the lacrimal gland, they normally start to flow. Tears continue to lubricate the eye until they are gathered in the superior and inferior puncta and drained into the common canaliculus. They then proceed to enter the lacrimal sac by passing through the Rosenmuller valve. Following the collection of tears in the lacrimal sac, the tears travel via the nasolacrimal duct, Hasner valve, and ultimately to the nasal cavity.Obstruction, stagnation, and infection are characteristic sequelae of dacryocystitis, causing edema and associated discharge. Communicating fistula formation between the skin and the lacrimal sac is an unusual sequela. Of the patients in the cited case series, 5.6% had a fistula, of which 83.3% had the lacrimal abscess burst spontaneously and 16.7% as a result of incision and drainage [3,4]. However, infective sinusitis, foreign bodies, facial traumas, and direct microbial inoculation are quite common etiologies. Methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus is the most commonly isolated germ from bacterial cultures of the purulent discharge (collected at the sac, fistula, or abscess surgical incision), followed by Streptococcus pneumoniae [5]. However, in our case, the culture sensitivity turned out to be sterile pus with no growth; this could be because the patient was loaded with antibiotics in various hospitals.Traumatic dacryocystitis is often due to a fracture of the bone surrounding the nasolacrimal duct, most often associated with fronto-nasoorbital-ethmoidal fractures. An untreated or incompletely treated blow-out fracture, or immediate tissue damage incurred with traumatic naso-orbital, orbital blow-out, and paranasal-maxillary fractures, can commonly lead to lacrimal obstruction, loss of the lacrimal pump mechanism, and nonspecific dacryocystitis [6,7]. Additionally, a few cases have been reported with implant loosening and migration of the mesh, subsequently causing an obstruction. In this case, lacrimal obstruction was caused by an orbital mesh placed after a successful reconstruction of a blow-out fracture repair two years ago, which was clearly evident in the high-resolution computed tomography (Figures 2-3). Damage to adjacent periorbital and paranasal structures may result from an overextended or improper reconstruction. Failure to place the mesh subperiosteally against the underlying bone may predispose it to migration. The orbital mesh should be placed subperiosteally, smoothing the edges to avoid damage to adjacent vital structures [8-10].

Conclusions

Though dacryocystitis is usually due to obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct, this can also be iatrogenic, especially in cases of fronto-orbital-ethmoidal fractures and medial wall of orbit fractures. Proper preoperative and postoperative assessment, avoiding overextension of orbital meshes, virtual surgical planning, dynamic navigation surgeries in orbital floor repair, and patient-specific implants can prevent such postoperative complications.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 22-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Swelling in the right lower eyelid region for two weeks.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual swelling in the right lower eyelid associated with pain and recurrent purulent discharge and epiphora since a reconstructive orbital floor repair performed two years ago.
- **Past Medical History:** History of road traffic accident leading to fronto-naso-orbito-ethmoidal fractures; treated with open reduction, internal fixation, orbital exploration, and floor reconstruction two years prior.
- **Personal History:** None provided.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** 6 cm x 4 cm erythematous, fluctuant, tender swelling below the medial canthus of the right eye with punctal leakage of purulent discharge. Normal vision without fever or conjunctivitis. Free and full extraocular eye movements.
- **Ancillary Tests:** High-resolution CT scan showed medial impingement and obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct from orbital mesh over-extension/migration; pus culture was sterile. During surgery, the mesh was found fused medially, obstructing the nasolacrimal duct.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Differential List:** Acute bacterial dacryocystitis, foreign body obstruction, traumatic nasolacrimal duct stenosis/obstruction, and implant-related complications. 
- **Evaluation and Rationale:**
  1. **Acute bacterial dacryocystitis**: Considered due to swelling, erythema, purulent discharge, and pain. However, pus culture was sterile, inconsistent with typical bacterial etiology.
  2. **Foreign body obstruction**: Considered due to prior trauma and symptoms of nasolacrimal obstruction, which CT confirmed as caused by orbital mesh migration. 
  3. **Traumatic nasolacrimal duct stenosis/obstruction**: Analysis highlighted prior trauma was appropriately repaired but later obstructed by iatrogenic factors (mesh migration) rather than primary trauma.
  4. **Implant-related complications**: Strongly suspected based on prior surgical history and CT imaging findings of orbital mesh impingement. Confirmed during surgical exploration.
- Less probable etiologies like sinusitis or recurrent trauma were excluded due to lack of fever, conjunctivitis, or recent injury.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Dacryocystitis secondary to obstruction caused by migrated orbital mesh implant following a previous orbital floor fracture repair. Diagnosis supported by clinical presentation of localized erythema, swelling with purulent discharge, confirmed medial mesh migration impinging on the nasolacrimal duct on CT, and successful surgical removal of overextended mesh with observed duct obstruction.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Dacryocystitis secondary to orbital mesh implant impingement.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

1. **Initial Presentation and History:**
   - The patient is a 22-year-old male with a two-week history of swelling in the right lower eyelid, accompanied by pain and recurrent purulent discharge and epiphora.
   - The swelling is gradual and associated with specific symptoms like discharge and epiphora, suggesting an inflammatory or infectious process.
   - Notably, these symptoms emerged after a reconstructive orbital floor repair performed two years ago, following a road traffic accident that caused fronto-naso-orbito-ethmoidal fractures.

2. **Clinical Examination Findings:**
   - Physical examination reveals a 6 cm x 4 cm erythematous, fluctuant, tender swelling below the medial canthus of the right eye.
   - There is punctal leakage of purulent discharge, indicating involvement of the lacrimal system.
   - Vision is normal, without fever or conjunctivitis, and extraocular eye movements are free and full, suggesting that the primary issue is localized to the eyelid and lacrimal system without widespread infection or neurological compromise.

3. **Past Medical History:**
   - Two years prior, the patient underwent open reduction, internal fixation, orbital exploration, and floor reconstruction due to fractures resulting from a road traffic accident.
   - This history is crucial as it suggests that the current symptoms may be related to the previous surgical intervention, possibly involving complications such as infection, mesh migration, or obstruction.

4. **Ancillary Tests:**
   - High-resolution CT scan shows medial impingement and obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct from orbital mesh over-extension or migration.
   - Pus culture was sterile, which is unusual for an infectious process and may indicate that the infection is chronic or that the causative organism is fastidious or not growing in standard culture conditions.
   - During surgery, the mesh was found fused medially, obstructing the nasolacrimal duct, confirming the CT findings.

5. **Differential Diagnosis:**
   - Given the history and exam findings, several diagnoses should be considered:
     - Orbital cellulitis: Although possible, the absence of fever and conjunctivitis makes it less likely, especially given the chronic nature of symptoms.
     - Dacryocystitis: Inflammation of the lacrimal sac, which can cause swelling and discharge, aligning with the patient's symptoms.
     - Mesh-related complications: Given the history of orbital floor reconstruction, complications related to the surgical mesh, such as extrusion, infection, or obstruction, are plausible.
     - Lacrimal duct obstruction: This could be due to scar tissue formation or direct obstruction by the mesh, as seen in the CT and during surgery.

6. **Diagnostic Confirmation:**
   - The CT scan findings and surgical observation of mesh fusion obstructing the nasolacrimal duct provide strong evidence for the cause of the patient's symptoms.
   - The sterile pus culture complicates the picture, suggesting that the inflammation may not be entirely infectious or that the infecting agent is not culturable under standard conditions.

7. **Final Consideration:**
   - Considering the patient's history, symptoms, and the findings from the CT scan and surgery, the most likely diagnosis is dacryocystitis secondary to nasolacrimal duct obstruction caused by migrated or overextended orbital floor reconstruction mesh.

### Answer:

Dacryocystitis due to nasolacrimal duct obstruction from orbital floor reconstruction meshmigration or overextension

6. **Diagnostic Confirmation:**

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The initial differential diagnosis included acute bacterial dacryocystitis, foreign body obstruction, traumatic nasolacrimal duct stenosis/obstruction, and implant-related complications.

<Step 2> Acute bacterial dacryocystitis was considered due to swelling, erythema, purulent discharge, and pain, but was ruled out because the pus culture was sterile, which is inconsistent with a typical bacterial etiology.
<Step 3> Foreign body obstruction was considered due to prior trauma and symptoms of nasolacrimal obstruction, and CT imaging confirmed orbital mesh migration as the cause.

<Step 4> Traumatic nasolacrimal duct stenosis/obstruction was analyzed, and it was determined that the prior trauma was appropriately repaired but later obstructed by iatrogenic factors, specifically mesh migration, rather than primary trauma.
<Step 5> Implant-related complications were strongly suspected based on prior surgical history and CT imaging findings of orbital mesh impingement, which was confirmed during surgical exploration.
<Step 6> Less probable etiologies, such as sinusitis or recurrent trauma, were excluded due to the absence of fever, conjunctivitis, or recent injury.
<Step 7> The final diagnosis was dacryocystitis secondary to obstruction caused by migrated orbital mesh implant following a previous orbital floor fracture repair.

<Step 8> The diagnosis was supported by clinical presentation of localized erythema, swelling with purulent discharge, CT findings of medial mesh migration impinging on the nasolacrimal duct, and successful surgical removal of the overextended mesh with observed duct obstruction.
